content.text: The asyncio Streams API provides a high-level, coroutine-based interface for reading from and writing to network connections and other byte-oriented I/O channels. Instead of dealing with raw sockets and callback-based transport objects directly, streams let you use
inside ordinary async functions — the same familiar style as the rest of asyncio code.

The Streams API sits at the top of asyncio's layered I/O model. Underneath it lie protocol objects, transport objects, and the event loop's low-level socket machinery. The Streams API wraps all of that into two objects:
— a handle you can push bytes into and drain
These two objects are obtained together through factory coroutines like
(server side). Once you have them, the entire network conversation is expressed as sequential
calls on those objects, which is far easier to reason about than managing transport callbacks manually.
Python's asyncio has multiple layers of I/O abstraction:

The low-level protocol/transport model is powerful but verbose: you implement a class, override callback methods, and mentally track state across multiple calls triggered by the event loop. The Streams API eliminates all of that by making the I/O sequential and awaitable. This makes it the right layer for:
Writing servers and clients for protocols not covered by existing libraries
Learning network programming with asyncio before diving into opinionated frameworks
still use the protocol/transport machinery. When you call
protocol and a TCP transport. Incoming bytes go into the
method and adds backpressure handling via
This means the event loop is still doing the actual I/O via the OS — the Streams API just gives you a coroutine-friendly interface to interact with the buffers the event loop fills and empties.
holds an internal byte buffer and maintains a set of
objects that coroutines waiting on it are attached to. When bytes arrive from the network, the event loop deposits them into the buffer and resolves any waiting Future, allowing the
on the read call to return.

— synchronous call. Puts bytes into the transport's write buffer immediately. Does not block, does not yield to the event loop.

— yields control to the event loop and waits until the write buffer drains below the "low water mark". This is the critical backpressure mechanism.
— waits until the underlying transport is actually closed. Use this after
gives the SSL context in TLS connections.
This is the most misunderstood part of the Streams API.
never blocks — it queues bytes into an internal buffer. If the consumer (e.g., the remote TCP peer) is slow, that buffer grows unboundedly unless you pause.
If the buffer is below the high water mark,
If the buffer is at or above the high water mark,
suspends your coroutine and yields to the event loop. When the transport drains to the low water mark, your coroutine resumes.
The standard pattern is to call
or after batches of writes in a tight loop:
in a loop that sends large amounts of data will eventually exhaust memory.
The diagram below shows the full layered stack and how the Streams API sits within it.---
is a coroutine that establishes a TCP connection and returns

import asyncio

async def fetch_time():
    reader, writer = await asyncio.open_connection('time.nist.gov', 13)
    try:
        data = await reader.read(1024)
        print(data.decode())
    finally:
        writer.close()
        await writer.wait_closed()

asyncio.run(fetch_time())
call does everything: resolves the hostname, creates the socket, performs the TCP handshake, and returns the two stream objects. The
block ensures the connection is always closed — even if a read raises an exception.
— sets the internal buffer limit for the
(default 65536 bytes = 64 KiB). This is not a maximum message size; it controls when
and when backpressure is applied to the sender.
for default SSL) to upgrade the connection to TLS.
— timeout in seconds for the TLS handshake.
starts a TCP server. The callback is called with

import asyncio

async def handle_client(reader: asyncio.StreamReader, writer: asyncio.StreamWriter):
    peer = writer.get_extra_info('peername')
    print(f"Connection from {peer}")

    try:
        while True:
            line = await reader.readline()
            if not line:          # EOF: client disconnected
                break
            if line == b'quit\r\n':
                writer.write(b'Goodbye\r\n')
                await writer.drain()
                break
            writer.write(b'Echo: ' + line)
            await writer.drain()
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        pass
    finally:
        writer.close()
        await writer.wait_closed()
        print(f"Connection closed: {peer}")

async def main():
    server = await asyncio.start_server(handle_client, '127.0.0.1', 8888)
    addrs = ', '.join(str(s.getsockname()) for s in server.sockets)
    print(f'Serving on {addrs}')
    async with server:
        await server.serve_forever()

asyncio.run(main())
A few things to observe here:
automatically wraps each call in a
, so each connected client gets its own concurrent task. There is no need to manually call
context manager is equivalent to calling
— runs until the current task is cancelled.
— waits for all existing handler tasks to finish.
Upgrading to TLS is a one-argument change. On the client:

import ssl

ssl_ctx = ssl.create_default_context()  # verifies server certificate
reader, writer = await asyncio.open_connection(
    'api.example.com', 443, ssl=ssl_ctx
)

ssl_ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_SERVER)
ssl_ctx.load_cert_chain('/path/to/cert.pem', '/path/to/key.pem')

server = await asyncio.start_server(
    handle_client, '0.0.0.0', 443, ssl=ssl_ctx
)
After this, reads and writes happen identically to non-TLS streams — the SSL handshake and record encryption/decryption are handled transparently by the transport layer.
plaintext connection to TLS mid-flight — useful for implementing STARTTLS protocols like SMTP and IMAP. Since Python 3.11, there is also
which does the same thing directly on a

# After initial plaintext handshake negotiates STARTTLS:
await writer.start_tls(ssl_ctx)
# All subsequent reads and writes are now encrypted

coroutines work identically to their TCP counterparts but operate on Unix domain socket paths instead of host/port pairs. These are significantly faster than TCP loopback for local IPC because they bypass the network stack entirely:

# Server
server = await asyncio.start_unix_server(handle_client, path='/tmp/myapp.sock')

# Client
reader, writer = await asyncio.open_unix_connection(path='/tmp/myapp.sock')
Real-world example — LLM streaming inference server

A common use case in Generative AI infrastructure is a streaming inference server: a client sends a prompt, and the server sends tokens back one by one as they are generated, rather than waiting for the full completion. The asyncio Streams API maps perfectly to this pattern.

import asyncio
import json
import struct

# Protocol: 4-byte big-endian length prefix, then UTF-8 JSON
HEADER = struct.Struct('>I')

async def read_message(reader: asyncio.StreamReader) -> dict:
    header = await reader.readexactly(4)
    length = HEADER.unpack(header)[0]
    body = await reader.readexactly(length)
    return json.loads(body)

async def write_message(writer: asyncio.StreamWriter, data: dict) -> None:
    body = json.dumps(data).encode()
    writer.write(HEADER.pack(len(body)) + body)
    await writer.drain()

async def handle_inference(reader: asyncio.StreamReader, writer: asyncio.StreamWriter):
    peer = writer.get_extra_info('peername')
    try:
        request = await asyncio.wait_for(read_message(reader), timeout=30.0)
        prompt = request.get('prompt', '')

        # Simulate streaming token generation
        for token in generate_tokens(prompt):   # your model's generator
            await write_message(writer, {'type': 'token', 'text': token})

        await write_message(writer, {'type': 'done'})

    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        await write_message(writer, {'type': 'error', 'msg': 'Request timeout'})
    except asyncio.IncompleteReadError:
        pass   # Client disconnected early
    finally:
        writer.close()
        await writer.wait_closed()

async def main():
    server = await asyncio.start_server(handle_inference, '0.0.0.0', 9000)
    async with server:
        await server.serve_forever()
Key design decisions in this example:
A 4-byte length-prefix framing protocol is used rather than newlines, because LLM tokens can contain arbitrary whitespace and JSON already contains
guarantees the header is complete before parsing;
prevents a slow client from holding a handler task open indefinitely.
The streaming nature means the client sees each token as soon as it is written, with no buffering delay, because

block or an async context manager.

EOF has arrived. If you call it before awaiting a read, there may still be bytes to consume. The correct EOF idiom is to check whether
bytes — it may return fewer if that's all the buffer holds at the moment. For fixed-size binary framing (length-prefix headers, struct data), always use
if the separator is not found before
bytes arrive. This is a protection against memory exhaustion. If you catch it and continue reading from the same reader, the stream is in an undefined state — the only correct response to

will block the entire event loop — all other connected clients stop receiving data. Use

A client that connects and then goes silent will hold a handler task open forever. Wrap read calls in
Reading fixed-size chunks and reassembling frames manually is error-prone. If your protocol is line-delimited,
Design a framing protocol for binary data.
. TCP is a byte stream — chunk boundaries are not preserved. Use a length-prefix (e.g., 4-byte header) or a delimiter (e.g.,
memory. If you accept large JSON blobs or binary payloads line-by-line, increase it. If you serve many clients on limited RAM, decrease it. Pass
Always log the client address at connection open and close. Without it, debugging multi-client servers is extremely difficult.
, then await all active handler tasks before terminating. This drains in-flight requests rather than killing them mid-response.
The asyncio Streams API is single-threaded within one event loop. Its throughput is bounded by:
The speed of user-space Python code in handlers
The overhead of the event loop itself (selector polling, callback dispatch)
The OS TCP stack for network I/O
processes each with their own event loop using
(available on Linux and macOS). Each process independently accepts connections on the same port, the kernel load-balances across them:

import socket, asyncio, os

async def main():
    sock = socket.socket()
    sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEPORT, 1)
    sock.bind(('0.0.0.0', 8888))
    server = await asyncio.start_server(handle_client, sock=sock)
    async with server:
        await server.serve_forever()

for _ in range(os.cpu_count()):
    if os.fork() == 0:
        asyncio.run(main())
is a drop-in replacement for the default asyncio event loop, built on
. For I/O-heavy workloads it typically delivers 2–4× more throughput with identical Streams API code:
Avoid large reads in a tight loop.
in a loop maximises throughput but starves other tasks. For long-lived streaming connections, intersperse
to yield control periodically, or use
per small message (e.g., one token at a time in a streaming LLM server) creates overhead from repeated syscalls. Batch multiple small writes and drain once:
— The Streams API itself. No additional package required for basic TCP/TLS.
— C-extension event loop based on libuv. Drop-in replacement. Install with
— HTTP/1.1 + WebSocket server and client built on asyncio. Uses streams internally. The standard choice for HTTP APIs.
— Async HTTP client with HTTP/2 support. Built on
(and therefore compatible with asyncio). Preferred over
for client-side HTTP in modern code.
— PostgreSQL client that talks directly over the asyncio Streams-equivalent layer (its own protocol implementation). Far faster than psycopg2 in async contexts.
— Compatibility layer over asyncio and trio. Its
are functionally equivalent to the asyncio Streams API but work with both event loop backends. Recommended for library code that should not be tied to asyncio specifically.
— An alternative async framework with a stricter structured concurrency model. Its networking API is conceptually similar to asyncio streams but uses
— Test harness for async code. Use
in test fixtures to spin up in-process test servers.
Full control over transport, custom framing at low cost

The key trade-off is between the Streams API (simple, asyncio-only, stdlib) and anyio (slightly more complex, but portable and increasingly the standard for library authors). For application code on asyncio, the Streams API is the right default. For library code, prefer anyio.
exists specifically to prevent memory exhaustion attacks. An adversary who sends bytes without a newline to a
to the maximum legitimate message size, not to an artificially large value.
will suspend forever if the client sends partial data and stalls. Wrap every read in
Bytes arriving from a network connection are untrusted. Parsing JSON, struct data, or length-prefixed frames should be wrapped in
to handle malformed input without crashing the handler.
Plaintext TCP exposes both the protocol and the data.
enforces hostname verification and certificate validation by default — do not disable these unless you are implementing mutual TLS with your own CA.
If a handler raises an unexpected exception, the
block ensures the socket is released. Without it, file descriptor leaks accumulate silently.
When the server task is cancelled (e.g., on SIGTERM), all handler tasks receive
. Catching it without re-raising will suppress the cancellation and prevent orderly shutdown. Either let it propagate or re-raise it explicitly:

is not itself a coroutine (it's synchronous), but interleaved writes from multiple coroutines to the same writer will produce corrupted output — bytes from two coroutines can be interleaved in the stream. If you must write to the same connection from multiple coroutines, guard with
objects that wrap asyncio's lower-level protocol/transport machinery.
is the server entry point — each handler coroutine runs as an independent
argument away from plain TCP, and works identically from the caller's perspective.
to every blocking read — never let a slow or silent client hold a handler task open indefinitely.
